the_one_thing.statement: When you change the order of the factors in a multiplication expression, the product stays the same — and arrays prove it visually.

critical_misconception.label: #13 — Order Changes Answer (Believing 3×4 ≠ 4×3). Students may have internalized from M11 that "3 rows of 4" and "4 columns of 3" are different descriptions and conclude they must produce different answers. *(Decision Q6)*
critical_misconception.why_critical: Order Changes Answer (Believing 3×4 ≠ 4×3). Students may have internalized from M11 that "3 rows of 4" and "4 columns of 3" are different descriptions and conclude they must produce different answers. *(Decision Q6)*

the_one_thing.success_indicator: Given a multiplication fact (e.g., 4 × 6 = 24), student can immediately state 6 × 4 = 24 without computing, AND can explain WHY using arrays (rotation or dual interpretation).
the_one_thing.biggest_risk: Students memorize "you can multiply in any order" as a rule without understanding WHY it works. If the visual proof (highlighting + rotation) doesn't land, students have a stated rule without a mental model — exactly the #20 pattern (Memorization Without Relationships).

student_facing_goal: Discover that you can multiply two numbers in any order and always get the same product — and prove it using arrays.
exit_check_tests: Write both expressions for an array using highlighting (dual interpretation fluency),
predict what happens after rotation (rotation understanding),
recognize whether two arrays are the same rotated or genuinely different (equivalence discrimination). (Decision Q5)
learning_goals: Represent an array situation with an equation with a symbol for the unknown number; Solve multiplication problems involving arrays.
Describe the commutative property of multiplication using arrays.

terms: array, row, column, expression, factor (all REINFORCED — M11 owned
vocabulary_staging.approach: Use naturally in callback; no re-teaching. These are active vocabulary from M11.

terms: All M11 terms active; product (REINFORCED — M9 owned
vocabulary_staging.approach: "Same product" becomes a repeated observation. No new vocabulary in Section 1 — consolidation phase.
Introduced in context: "That's a strategy — a plan for solving a problem. If you know one fact, you have a strategy to find its partner."

terms: you can multiply in any order
vocabulary_staging.approach: Guide names the pattern after students have seen multiple proofs: "What you discovered has a name. Mathematicians call this the commutative property. Since you can multiply in any order and get the same answer we can call these turn-around facts. "
Used in problem stems and feedback. No new vocabulary.
Used in question contexts and feedback.
Expect natural use. Reflection options reference core concepts using established terms.

misconceptions.description: Student sees 3 × 4 = 12 and insists 4 × 3 must be different (or hesitates/recomputes instead of applying the property). When shown a rotated array, student says "that's a different array" or "you can't just switch the numbers." In Exit Check, student correctly identifies both expressions for an array but doesn't connect them to the same product.

Student can state "you can multiply in any order" but cannot explain why, cannot connect the rule to an array visual, and cannot apply it to unfamiliar facts. Student treats the commutative property as another rule to memorize rather than a structural feature of multiplication they've proven.

core_concepts: Same array can be described two ways (rows or columns) — M11 consolidation
Both descriptions produce expressions that evaluate to the same product — M12 NEW
Highlighting (row emphasis → column emphasis) demonstrates this visually without adding or removing items
Guide explicitly names the pattern: "Same array, same items, same product — no matter which way you describe it"
Rotation animation: a 3×4 array physically turns 90° to become a 4×3 array
Items don't change — what were rows become columns and vice versa
This is a DIFFERENT proof of the same property: rotation proves that 3×4 and 4×3 are related transformations of the same arrangement
Hybrid understanding: highlighting shows "two ways to read ONE array"; rotation shows "two arrays that are the SAME array turned"
Both proofs lead to the same conclusion: order doesn't change the product
Name the discovery: "You can multiply in any order" (primary) / "commutative property" (exposure, 1-2 uses) (Decision Q2)
Strategic application: if you know one fact, you know its partner without computing
Mixed tasks: highlighting, rotation prediction, property application ("If 6 × 4 = 24, what's 4 × 6?")
Equivalence discrimination: distinguish rotated arrays (same total, same items) from genuinely different arrays (same total, different dimensions — e.g., 4×6 vs 3×8, both = 24)
